curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III Volume 2, Learning Module 1

drills.los_text: describe the roles of equities in the overall portfolio

questions.stem: The reading identifies four primary roles equities play in an overall portfolio. Which of the following is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: Guaranteed principal protection.
options.B: Capital appreciation.
options.C: Dividend income.

rationales.A: Correct. Equities do not offer guaranteed principal protection. The four roles the reading identifies are: (1) capital appreciation, (2) dividend income, (3) diversification with other asset classes, and (4) potential hedge against inflation. Client considerations also drive equity inclusion decisions.
rationales.B: This is one of the four.
rationales.C: This is one of the four.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities in a Portfolio

questions.stem: Exhibit 1 in the reading (Credit Suisse Global Investment Returns Yearbook 2023) shows real returns 1900–2022. It supports the conclusion that *most likely*:

options.A: bonds outperformed equities globally across the 1900–2022 period.
options.B: equities outperformed both bonds and bills across the world during 1900–2022, with world equity real returns around the mid-single digits annualized.
options.C: equity real returns were negative in most countries.

rationales.A: This is the opposite of what Exhibit 1 shows.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states long-term returns on equities, driven predominantly by capital appreciation, have historically been among the highest among major asset classes. Equities outperformed both bonds and bills across the world during 1900–2022. World equity real return is shown around 5.2% annualized in Exhibit 1.
rationales.C: Equity real returns were positive across most countries shown.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — Exhibit 1 real returns

questions.stem: Capital appreciation of equities typically occurs *most likely* in:

options.A: companies with declining earnings, cash flows, or revenues.
options.B: only in mature dividend-paying companies.
options.C: companies with growth in earnings, cash flows, and/or revenues.

rationales.A: Declining fundamentals typically depress rather than appreciate share price.
rationales.B: Growth-oriented small companies are also identified.
rationales.C:
Full statement: companies with growth in earnings, cash flows, and/or revenues — as well as in companies with competitive success — including both growth-oriented small technology companies and large, mature companies where management successfully maintains profitability.

Correct. The reading states capital appreciation occurs when investing in companies with growth in earnings, cash flows, and/or revenues — as well as competitive success. Capital appreciation can occur in growth-oriented companies (small technology companies) as well as in large, mature companies where management successfully maintains profitability. Equities also tend to outperform during periods of strong economic growth.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — capital appreciation

questions.stem: The reading notes the S&P 500 dividend yield since 1990 has been in the *most likely* range of:

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states that since 1990, the dividend yield on the S&P 500 has been in the 1%–3% range. Thus the effect of dividends can clearly be significant during periods of weak equity market performance, such as during the first decade of the 21st century when price returns were negative. Dividend yield may vary considerably by sector.
rationales.B: This is above the range the reading identifies.
rationales.C: This is far above post-1990 S&P 500 dividend yields.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — S&P 500 dividend yield range

questions.stem: The reading identifies three sources of dividend income for equity portfolios. Which is *least likely* one of the three?

options.A: Regular dividends on common stock.
options.B: Interest payments on the underlying company's bonds.
options.C: Special dividends and optional stock dividends.

rationales.A: Regular common dividends are the primary source.
rationales.B: Correct. Interest on a company's bonds is bond income, not equity income. The reading identifies: (1) regular dividends on common stock, (2) preferred dividends for preferred shareholders, (3) special dividends when companies distribute excess cash, and (4) optional stock dividends where shareholders elect cash or new shares (the optionality has value).
rationales.C: Special and optional stock dividends are discussed.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — dividend sources

questions.stem: The reading argues that although equities can provide diversification benefits when combined with other asset classes, an important caveat is that *most likely*:

options.A: correlations are stable across time and market environments.
options.B: diversification benefits are guaranteed regardless of market conditions.
options.C: correlations are not constant over time; during periods of market crisis, correlations across asset classes and among equities themselves often increase and reduce the benefit of diversification.

rationales.A: The reading warns explicitly that correlations shift with regime.
rationales.B: Diversification benefits weaken precisely when they are most needed.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading states correlations are not constant over time. During a long historical period the correlation between two asset classes may be low, but in any given period it can differ from the long-term average. During periods of market crisis, correlations across asset classes and among equities often increase and reduce diversification benefits. Volatility of asset class returns may also vary over time.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — diversification caveats

questions.stem: The reading's assessment of equities as an *inflation hedge* is *most likely*:

options.A: the ability of equities to hedge inflation varies — certain sectors/companies may pass on costs to customers or benefit from commodity price rises, but empirical evidence is mixed.
options.B: equities are a reliable inflation hedge under all inflationary regimes.
options.C: equities are only negatively correlated with inflation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the ability of equities to hedge inflation varies — certain sectors/companies may pass on costs to customers or benefit from commodity price rises, but empirical evidence is mixed; in severe inflation periods (annual rate over 5%), real equity returns and inflation have been *negatively* correlated, so the asset class's efficacy as an inflation hedge may fail when it is most needed.

Correct. The reading states real equity returns and inflation have positive correlation over the long term but the degree varies by country and time period. For severe inflationary periods (annual inflation rate over 5%), studies have shown real returns on equities and inflation have been negatively correlated — the asset class's efficacy as an inflation hedge may fail when most needed. Some companies (broad commodity producers, or firms able to pass through input costs) can hedge inflation better.
rationales.B: Empirical evidence is mixed, not confirmatory.
rationales.C: The long-term correlation is positive; short-term/high-inflation is negative.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — inflation hedge

questions.stem: The reading identifies seven investment objectives and constraints for including equities in a client's portfolio. Which of the following is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: Risk objective, return objective, liquidity requirement, time horizon.
options.B: Trading commission tier of the client's brokerage account.
options.C: Tax concerns, legal and regulatory factors, unique circumstances.

rationales.A: These are four of the seven.
rationales.B: Correct. Brokerage commission tiers are not among the seven considerations. The seven are: (1) risk objective, (2) return objective, (3) liquidity requirement, (4) time horizon, (5) tax concerns, (6) legal and regulatory factors, and (7) unique circumstances (which may include ESG issues or religious preferences).
rationales.C: These are three of the seven.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — IPS considerations

questions.stem: Historically, ESG approaches used by portfolio managers have largely represented *most likely*:

options.A: activist investing exclusively.
options.B: purely quantitative factor investing.
options.C: negative (exclusionary) screening and positive (best-in-class) screening.

rationales.A: Activist investing is a separate concept.
rationales.B: Factor investing is a separate concept.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading states historically ESG approaches have largely represented negative screening (excluding certain sectors/companies that deviate from accepted standards) and positive screening or best-in-class approaches (identifying companies scoring most favorably on ESG risks/opportunities). For example, negative screening excludes oil/gas producers; positive screening overweights companies with strong governance practices such as an independent board chair.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — ESG approaches historically

questions.stem: As of 2020 (GSIA), the largest sustainable investment strategy globally is *most likely*:

options.A: ESG integration — the inclusion of ESG considerations in financial analysis and investment decisions.
options.B: negative screening.
options.C: positive screening.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states rather than screening, as of 2020, the largest sustainable investment strategy globally was ESG integration, which is the inclusion of ESG considerations in financial analysis and investment decisions (GSIA 2020). The goals of ESG integration are to reduce financial risks and/or enhance financial returns by identifying and valuing risks or opportunities that are not typically identified and valued.
rationales.B: Negative screening was the historical approach, no longer the largest.
rationales.C: Positive screening is smaller than ESG integration.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — ESG integration

questions.stem: A risk-averse and conservative equity investor's preferences *most likely* align with:

options.A: small, high-growth technology companies.
options.B: well-established companies with strong and stable cash flow that pay meaningful dividends.
options.C: highly volatile emerging market equities.

rationales.A: Growth technology names carry higher volatility than a conservative investor typically prefers.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states a risk-averse and conservative investor may prefer some exposure to well-established companies with strong and stable cash flow that pay meaningful dividends. Conversely, a growth-oriented investor with aggressive risk tolerance may prefer smaller companies with greater growth potential. Portfolio managers can adapt specific factors (growth potential, income generation, risk/return volatility, macroeconomic sensitivity) to an investor's goals and tolerance.
rationales.C: This is inconsistent with risk aversion.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — client considerations

questions.stem: For a client concerned about *inflation* who wishes to preserve purchasing power, the reading recommends *most likely*:

options.A: For inflation protection, the reading recommends concentrating in long-duration nominal bonds.
options.B: For inflation protection, the reading recommends holding only cash equivalents.
options.C: companies that can successfully pass on higher input costs to customers, or commodity producers that may benefit from rising commodity prices.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Long-duration nominal bonds are among the assets MOST damaged by inflation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Cash loses purchasing power in inflation; pricing-power companies and commodity producers are the equity answer.
rationales.C:
Full statement: companies that can successfully pass on higher input costs to customers, or commodity producers that may benefit from rising commodity prices — recognizing that equities' inflation-hedge efficacy is mixed at best.

Correct. The reading's inflation-hedge discussion (and the Huber portfolio-manager example) notes: companies successful at passing along higher input costs (raw materials, energy, wages) to customers can protect margins; commodity producers (oil, industrial metals) benefit directly from commodity price rises. Certain sectors provide some protection though the asset class overall is mixed as an inflation hedge.

questions.curriculum_ref: The Roles of Equities — inflation hedge, Huber example

drills.los_text: describe how an equity manager's investment universe can be segmented

questions.stem: The reading identifies three primary approaches to segmenting the equity investment universe. These are *most likely*:

options.A: size/style, geography, and economic activity.
options.B: growth, value, and blend.
options.C: large-cap, mid-cap, and small-cap.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading identifies three primary segmentation approaches: (1) size and style, (2) geography, and (3) economic activity. Each has advantages and disadvantages, and they can be combined (e.g., MSCI Europe Large Cap Growth Index combines geography, size, and style).
rationales.B: These are style dimensions, part of one segmentation approach.
rationales.C: These are size categories, part of one segmentation approach.

questions.stem: In size/style segmentation, *style* is *most likely* determined by:

options.A: In size/style segmentation, style is determined by market capitalization alone.
options.B: a scoring system incorporating multiple metrics or ratios such as price-to-book, price-to-earnings, earnings growth, dividend yield, and book value growth.
options.C: Style is determined by the geographic domicile of the company.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Capitalization defines the SIZE axis; style needs valuation and growth metrics.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a scoring system incorporating multiple metrics or ratios such as price-to-book, price-to-earnings, earnings growth, dividend yield, and book value growth — scored individually, weighted, and aggregated to produce a composite score positioning the stock along the value-growth spectrum.

Correct. The reading states style is often determined through a scoring system that incorporates multiple metrics or ratios (P/B, P/E, earnings growth, dividend yield, book value growth). These metrics are scored individually for each company, assigned weights, and aggregated to produce a composite score determining where the stock falls along the value-growth spectrum. A combination of growth and value is not uncommon, particularly for large corporations with both mature and higher-growth business lines.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Domicile is the geographic approach; style scoring blends price-to-book, price-to-earnings, earnings growth, and dividend yield.

questions.stem: The reading identifies four advantages of segmentation by size/style. Which of the following is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: Ability to construct a portfolio reflecting desired risk, return, and income characteristics in a straightforward manner.
options.B: Diversification across economic sectors or industries.
options.C: Perfect stability of size/style classification over time.

rationales.A: This is one of the advantages.
rationales.B: This is one of the advantages.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading explicitly identifies as a *disadvantage* that size/style categories may change over time and may be defined differently among investors. The four advantages are: (1) straightforward portfolio construction reflecting desired characteristics, (2) diversification across sectors/industries, (3) ability to construct performance benchmarks for specific size/style segments, and (4) accommodation of a company's maturity and changing growth/value orientation (e.g., IPO small-cap growth → mature large-cap value).

questions.curriculum_ref: Segmentation by Size and Style — advantages/disadvantages

questions.stem: The reading illustrates how companies migrate along the size/style spectrum. An IPO of a small technology company is *most likely* initially classified as:

options.A: Small Cap Growth, which may over time move to Mid or Large Cap, and possibly shift from growth to value or a growth-and-income combination.
options.B: Large Cap Value.
options.C: always remains Small Cap Growth throughout its lifecycle.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states many companies that undertake an IPO are small and in a growth phase, and thus may fall in the small-cap growth category. If these companies can successfully grow, their size may move to mid-cap or large cap, while their style may shift from high growth to value or a combination (growth and income stock). Over the life cycle, investor preferences may shift increasingly from capital appreciation to dividend income.
rationales.B: Newly IPO'd tech companies are typically not large-cap or value.
rationales.C: The reading identifies the migration.

questions.curriculum_ref: Segmentation by Size and Style — company life cycle

questions.stem: Geographic segmentation typically categorizes markets as *most likely*:

options.A: American, European, and Asian only.
options.B: developed markets, emerging markets, and frontier markets.
options.C: developed markets only.

rationales.A: These are regions, not the market-tier classification.
rationales.B:
Full statement: developed markets, emerging markets, and frontier markets — with MSCI (and similar providers like FTSE, S&P, Russell) using a holistic framework considering economic development, size and liquidity, and accessibility criteria (openness to foreign equity ownership).

Correct. The reading states common geographic categories are developed markets, emerging markets, and frontier markets. Exhibit 6 shows MSCI's classification. MSCI classifies countries according to a holistic framework that considers economic development, size and liquidity, and accessibility criteria such as openness to foreign equity ownership. FTSE, S&P, and Russell provide similar indexes.
rationales.C: Emerging and frontier markets are also key categories.

questions.stem: The reading warns that a key weakness of geographic segmentation is *most likely*:

options.A: geographic indexes are always perfectly representative of local economic activity.
options.B: currency risk is eliminated by geographic segmentation.
options.C: investing in a specific market may provide lower-than-expected exposure to that market.

rationales.A: This is what the reading warns is not always the case.
rationales.B: Currency risk is actually introduced, not eliminated, by international investing.
rationales.C:
Full statement: investing in a specific market may provide lower-than-expected exposure to that market — for example, Nestle, Roche, and Novartis together account for over half of the MSCI Switzerland Index, but Switzerland accounts for less than 2% of each company's sales.

Correct. The reading identifies this specific weakness: investing in a specific market index may provide lower-than-expected exposure to that market. Nestle, Roche, and Novartis together account for over half of the MSCI Switzerland Index, but Switzerland accounts for less than 2% of each company's sales — so 'Swiss equity exposure' via the index is largely global multinational exposure. Another weakness is potential currency risk when investing in different global equity markets.

questions.stem: The reading identifies four primary commercial global classification systems for economic-activity segmentation. Which of the following is *least likely* among them?

options.A: Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) and North American Industry Classification System (NAICS).
options.B: Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) and Industrial Classification Benchmark (ICB).
options.C: Thomson Reuters Business Classification (TRBC) and Russell Global Sectors Classification (RGS).

rationales.A: Correct. SIC and NAICS are US government classification systems, not among the four primary commercial global systems the reading identifies. The four are: (1) GICS, (2) ICB, (3) TRBC, and (4) RGS.
rationales.B: GICS and ICB are two of the four commercial systems.
rationales.C: TRBC and RGS are two of the four commercial systems.

questions.stem: The Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) structure includes *most likely*:

options.A: 5 Sectors → 15 Groups → 30 Industries → 60 Sub-Industries.
options.B: 11 Sectors → 24 Industry Groups → 68 Industries → 157 Sub-Industries.
options.C: 20 Sectors → 40 Industry Groups → 100 Industries → 300 Sub-Industries.

rationales.A: These figures are too low.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading's Exhibit 7: GICS has 11 Sectors (1st level), 24 Industry Groups (2nd level), 68 Industries (3rd level), and 157 Sub-Industries (4th level). The example illustrates: Consumer Discretionary sector → Automobiles and Components industry group → Automobiles industry → Motorcycle Manufacturers sub-industry.
rationales.C: These figures are too high.

questions.curriculum_ref: Segmentation by Economic Activity — GICS structure

questions.stem: The Industrial Classification Benchmark (ICB) uses the terminology *most likely*:

options.A: 10 Sectors → 40 Industries → 100 Sub-Sectors.
options.B: 11 Sectors → 24 Industry Groups → 68 Industries → 157 Sub-Industries.
options.C: 10 Industries → 19 Super Sectors → 41 Sectors → 114 Sub-Sectors.

rationales.A: These are not the ICB figures.
rationales.B: These are the GICS figures.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading's Exhibit 7, ICB is structured as: 10 Industries (1st level), 19 Super Sectors (2nd), 41 Sectors (3rd), 114 Sub-Sectors (4th). Note that ICB uses 'Industries' at the highest level (analogous to GICS 'Sectors'), which can be confusing when comparing systems.

questions.curriculum_ref: Segmentation by Economic Activity — ICB structure

questions.stem: A key *disadvantage* of segmentation by economic activity is *most likely*:

options.A: the business activities of companies.
options.B: excessive standardization.
options.C: the requirement to update classifications every hour.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the business activities of companies — particularly large ones — may include more than one industry or sub-industry, so classification may not fully capture the diversified nature of large conglomerates.

Correct. The reading states the key disadvantage of segmentation by economic activity is that the business activities of companies — particularly large ones — may include more than one industry or sub-industry. A diversified multinational may operate across multiple GICS sectors, making single-classification imperfect.
rationales.B: Standardization is actually one of the advantages.
rationales.C: Not the reading's disadvantage.

questions.stem: The reading's recommendation for a portfolio manager launching a fund focused on the *Chinese robotics industry* with rapidly accelerating earnings is *most likely* to use:

options.A: For the Chinese robotics fund, geographic segmentation alone is the recommended framework.
options.B: size/style segmentation with growth-oriented style (size depending on the company), since the fund's rapid-earnings-growth focus best aligns with growth style.
options.C: For the fund, economic activity segmentation alone captures the relevant characteristics.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Geography is already fixed by the mandate — it adds no discriminating power.
rationales.B:
Full statement: size/style segmentation with growth-oriented style (size depending on the company), since the fund's rapid-earnings-growth focus best aligns with growth style; geography and economic activity segmentation are less appropriate given the similar geographic and industry composition of the target industry.

Correct. Per the reading's example, based on the desired strategy to invest in companies with rapidly accelerating earnings, the manager would most likely segment by size/style — using an investment style that reflects growth, with size (large, mid, or small cap) depending on the company. Other segmentation approaches (geography and economic activity) would be less appropriate given the similar geographic and industry composition of the Chinese robotics industry.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Sector segmentation misses the accelerating-earnings dimension; size/style with a GROWTH orientation fits the fund's character.

questions.stem: Segmentation of equity *indexes* frequently reflects *combinations* of multiple approaches. An example the reading cites is *most likely*:

options.A: The reading's example of a multi-approach index combination is the S&P 500 alone.
options.B: The reading's example is the Dow Jones Industrial Average.
options.C: the MSCI Europe Large Cap Growth Index and the MSCI World Small Cap Value Index, which combine geographic, size, and style dimensions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The S&P 500 reflects a single approach — selective large-cap US.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The DJIA is a 30-stock price-weighted index; the cited combinations are MSCI Europe Large Cap Growth and MSCI World Small Cap Value, blending geography, size, and style.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading identifies these indexes as examples combining geography, size, and style: MSCI Europe Large Cap Growth Index, MSCI World Small Cap Value Index, MSCI Emerging Markets Large Cap Growth Index, and MSCI Latin America Midcap Index. This combination also sometimes applies to individual countries, particularly large developed markets.

questions.curriculum_ref: Segmentation of Equity Indexes and Benchmarks

questions.stem: Sector-based equity indexes typically composed of companies in different countries include *most likely*:

options.A: the S&P Global Natural Resources Index (90 largest natural resources/commodities companies across agribusiness, energy, and metals and mining) and the MSCI World Energy Index (large-cap and mid-cap oil and natural gas).
options.B: Sector-based equity indexes are constructed only within single countries.
options.C: Multi-country indexes exist only for whole markets, never for individual sectors.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading cites these as examples of sector indexes that are global in scope: S&P Global Natural Resources Index (90 largest publicly traded companies in natural resources/commodities across agribusiness, energy, and metals and mining); MSCI World Energy Index (large-cap and mid-cap segments of publicly traded oil and natural gas in developed markets); Refinitiv Global Financials Index (100 largest global financial services). Because many sectors are global, industry-focused indexes are often global.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Sector indexes routinely cross borders.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The S&P Global Natural Resources Index — 90 companies spanning agribusiness, energy, and metals worldwide — is the cited example.

questions.curriculum_ref: Segmentation of Equity Indexes — sector indexes

drills.los_text: describe the types of income and costs associated with owning and managing an equity portfolio and their potential effects on portfolio performance

questions.stem: The reading identifies the *primary source of income* for equity portfolios as *most likely*:

options.A: capital gains on trades.
options.B: dividends — both regular and special.
options.C: interest on bond holdings.

rationales.A: Capital gains are appreciation, not income.
rationales.B:
Full statement: dividends — both regular and special — supplemented by securities lending fees, option premiums (covered calls, cash-covered puts), and dividend-capture strategies.

Correct. The reading states dividends are the primary source of income for equity portfolios. Beyond regular dividends, portfolios may receive special dividends (when companies distribute excess cash) and optional stock dividends (shareholders elect cash or new shares). Additional income sources include securities lending fees/interest, dividend capture strategies, and option writing (covered calls, cash-covered puts).
rationales.C: Bond interest is not equity portfolio income.

questions.curriculum_ref: Income Associated with Owning and Managing an Equity Portfolio

questions.stem: Securities lending fees for stock loans in *developed markets* on typical shares are *most likely*:

options.A: 5%–10% annualized on typical loans.
options.B: 25%–100% annualized on typical loans.
options.C: roughly 0.2%–0.5% annualized on typical loans.

rationales.A: This range applies to 'specials', not typical DM stocks.
rationales.B: This is the extreme demand range for scarce 'specials'.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading states most stock loans in developed markets earn a modest fee, approximately 0.2%–0.5% on an annualized basis. In emerging markets, fees are typically higher — often 1%–2% annualized for large-cap stocks. Certain 'specials' (in high demand for borrowing) can earn substantially higher fees (5%–15% annualized, or 25%–100% in extreme demand), but such high fees don't normally persist long.

questions.curriculum_ref: Securities Lending Income — DM fee ranges

questions.stem: The reading notes that when a stock is loaned, dividends paid on the stock are *most likely*:

options.A: 'manufactured' by the stock borrower for the lender — the borrower ensures the lender is compensated for any dividends the lender would have received had the stock not been loaned.
options.B: kept by the stock borrower with no compensation to the lender.
options.C: cancelled during the loan period.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states dividends on loaned stock are 'manufactured' by the stock borrower for the stock lender — that is, the stock borrower ensures the stock lender is compensated for any dividends that the lender would have received had the stock not been loaned. This preserves the lender's economic dividend income.
rationales.B: The reading identifies compensation, not retention by borrower.
rationales.C: Dividends are not cancelled by loans.

questions.stem: The reading identifies *index funds* as frequent stock lenders because *most likely*:

options.A: Index funds lend stocks frequently because regulation requires them to do so.
options.B: of their large, long-term holdings in stocks, and because index-fund managers are normally not concerned that borrowed stock used for short-selling might depress the corresponding equity prices.
options.C: Index funds lend stocks because they need external cash to fund operations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. No lending mandate exists; the practice is commercial.
rationales.B:
Full statement: of their large, long-term holdings in stocks, and because index-fund managers are normally not concerned that borrowed stock used for short-selling might depress the corresponding equity prices — index funds merely seek to replicate benchmark performance.

Correct. The reading states index funds are frequent stock lenders because of their large, long-term holdings in stocks. In addition, because index funds merely seek to replicate the performance of an index, portfolio managers of these funds are normally not concerned that borrowed stock used for short-selling purposes might decrease the prices of the corresponding equities. Large actively managed pension funds, endowments, and institutional investors are also frequent lenders (though more concerned with short-selling impact).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Operational cash is not the driver — large, stable long-term holdings and indifference to short-sale price pressure make lending attractive income.

questions.curriculum_ref: Securities Lending — index funds as lenders

questions.stem: The *dividend capture* strategy is *most likely* described as:

options.A: selling before ex-dividend to avoid taxes.
options.B: holding stocks indefinitely to capture all future dividends.
options.C: purchasing stocks just before their ex-dividend dates, holding through the ex-dividend date to earn the dividend right, and subsequently selling the shares.

rationales.A: This is the opposite of dividend capture.
rationales.B: This describes buy-and-hold, not dividend capture.
rationales.C:
Full statement: purchasing stocks just before their ex-dividend dates, holding through the ex-dividend date to earn the dividend right, and subsequently selling the shares — recognizing that in theory the share price should decline by the dividend amount, though the actual movement varies based on taxes, supply/demand, and market moves.

Correct. The reading defines dividend capture as: purchase stocks just before ex-dividend dates → hold through ex-dividend date to earn dividend right → subsequently sell shares. In theory, the share price should decrease by the dividend value after going ex-dividend. In this way, capturing dividends increases portfolio income, though the portfolio would in theory experience capital losses of similar magnitude. Actual share price movement may vary from theory due to income tax considerations, stock-specific supply/demand, and general market moves around the ex-dividend date.

questions.stem: A *covered call* strategy generates income but *most likely* has the risk profile characteristic of:

options.A: limiting the upside from share price appreciation of the underlying shares, in exchange for the call premium received.
options.B: unlimited upside participation.
options.C: eliminating downside risk entirely.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states writing a covered call (portfolio manager owns underlying stock and sells a call option on it) generates income through the option premium, but clearly the risk profile is altered. Writing a covered call would limit the upside from share price appreciation of the underlying shares — if the stock rises above the strike, the shares are called away at the strike price. A cash-covered put similarly generates income while requiring the writer to deposit cash equal to the exercise price.
rationales.B: Selling the call caps the upside.
rationales.C: Downside risk on the stock remains.

questions.stem: *Management fees* for equity portfolios are *most likely* determined as:

options.A: a fixed dollar amount regardless of portfolio value.
options.B: an ad-valorem fee — a percentage of the funds under management at regular intervals.
options.C: a percentage of realized capital gains only.

rationales.A: Fixed dollar fees are not typical for equity portfolios.
rationales.B:
Full statement: an ad-valorem fee — a percentage of the funds under management at regular intervals — typically higher for actively managed portfolios (to fund research) and lower for index-based portfolios (lower direct research and portfolio management costs).

Correct. The reading states management fees are typically determined as a percentage of the funds under management (an ad-valorem fee) at regular intervals. For actively managed portfolios, fees balance being high enough to fund research and low enough not to detract too much from returns. Active management fees include direct costs of research (analyst/PM remuneration, expenses) and portfolio management (software, trade processing, compliance). Index-based management fees are typically low due to lower research and management costs.
rationales.C: Fees on gains alone describe performance fees.

questions.stem: *Performance fees* in equity portfolios are *most likely*:

options.A: identical to management fees, paid quarterly regardless of returns.
options.B: waived on all hedge fund performance.
options.C: generally associated with hedge funds and long/short portfolios rather than long-only.

rationales.A: Performance fees and management fees are distinct.
rationales.B: Performance fees are not waived — they are collected when performance is achieved.
rationales.C:
Full statement: generally associated with hedge funds and long/short portfolios rather than long-only; often 10%–20% of capital appreciation above a stated annual threshold (e.g., 8%); typically 'upwards only' (fees are earned when performance objectives are met, but investors are not reimbursed when performance is negative), and typically calculated with a high-water mark to protect clients from paying twice for the same performance.

Correct. The reading states performance fees (incentive fees) are generally associated with hedge funds and long/short equity portfolios rather than long-only. A typical structure is 10%–20% of any capital appreciation above a stated annual absolute return threshold (e.g., 8%). Fees tend to be 'upwards only' — earned when objectives are met but not reimbursed when negative (though can be reduced following poor performance). Fee calculations reflect high-water marks — the highest value net of fees the fund has reached.

questions.stem: The *high-water mark* protects clients from *most likely*:

options.A: paying twice for the same performance.
options.B: regulatory penalties.
options.C: paying any management fees.

rationales.A:
Full statement: paying twice for the same performance — if a fund performed well in year 1 (earning a performance fee), then fell in year 2, then partially recovered in year 3 to a value below the year-1 high-water mark, no performance fee would be earned in year 3 because the value did not exceed the high-water mark.

Correct. The reading's high-water mark example: if a fund performed well in a year (earning a performance fee), and then the fund's value fell the following year, no performance fee would be payable. Then if the fund's value increased in the third year to a point just below the value achieved at the end of the first year, no performance fee would be earned because the fund's value did not exceed the high-water mark. This basic structure is used by many alternative investment funds and partnerships, including hedge funds.
rationales.B: High-water marks are contractual, not regulatory.
rationales.C: Management fees continue regardless.

questions.stem: The reading identifies four components of *trading costs* for equity portfolios. Which of the following is *least likely* among them?

options.A: Explicit costs (commissions, fees, taxes) and implicit costs (bid-ask spread, market impact).
options.B: Performance-fee accruals.
options.C: Delay costs (slippage) arising from the inability to complete desired trades immediately due to order size or lack of market liquidity.

rationales.A: Explicit and implicit costs are two components.
rationales.B: Correct. Performance-fee accruals are portfolio-management costs, not trading costs. The reading identifies trading costs as: (1) explicit costs (commissions, fees, taxes), (2) implicit costs (bid-ask spread, market impact — the effect of a trade on transaction prices), and (3) delay costs/slippage (inability to complete desired trades immediately due to order size or lack of market liquidity).
rationales.C: Delay costs are a component.

questions.stem: The reading notes that unlike management fees, the total cost of trading is *most likely*:

options.A: reported quarterly to all investors in itemized form.
options.B: always higher than management fees.
options.C: generally not revealed to the investor — rather, trading costs are incorporated into the portfolio's total return and presented as overall performance data.

rationales.A: This is not standard practice.
rationales.B: The relative size varies.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading states unlike many other equity portfolio costs such as management fees, the total cost of trading is generally not revealed to the investor. Rather, trading costs are incorporated into a portfolio's total return and presented as overall performance data. This makes trading costs less visible than explicit fee items.

questions.stem: The reading identifies a *hidden cost* faced by index funds — *most likely*:

options.A: predatory trading — where predatory traders purchase (or sell short) shares prior to their effective inclusion (or deletion) from an index, resulting in price movement and profit for the predatory trader at the expense of the index fund.
options.B: Index funds face no hidden costs given their transparency and low turnover.
options.C: The hidden cost identified is that index funds pay higher commissions than active funds.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading states index funds face a 'hidden' cost from potential predatory trading. A predatory trader may purchase (or sell short) shares prior to their effective inclusion (or deletion) from an index, resulting in price movement and potential profit for the predatory trader. Such strategies can be regarded as a cost to index-fund investors — albeit a cost that is not necessarily evident to a portfolio manager or investor.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Transparency is the vulnerability — predictable trades invite predation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Commissions are LOWER for index funds; the hidden cost is predatory trading ahead of announced inclusions and deletions.

questions.curriculum_ref: Investment Approaches and Effects on Costs — predatory trading

questions.stem: *Momentum* strategies and *deep value* strategies differ in their trading-cost characteristics because *most likely*:

options.A: Momentum and deep value strategies have identical trading cost profiles, since both trade the same universe of listed equities through the same brokers and venues.
options.B: momentum strategies 'demand liquidity' (buying stocks already rising, selling those already falling) so tend to have higher trading costs, while deep value strategies more likely 'provide liquidity' to the market (buying significantly undervalued stocks) so tend to have lower trading costs.
options.C: Deep value strategies always incur higher trading costs than momentum strategies, because unloved stocks trade with wider spreads that dominate any liquidity-demand effect.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Their liquidity postures differ fundamentally.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states some active investing approaches 'demand liquidity' from the market — for example, in a momentum strategy the investor seeks to buy shares already rising in price (or sell those already falling). Deep value strategies (involving stocks deemed significantly undervalued) are more likely to 'provide liquidity' to the market. Investment strategies that involve frequent trading and demand liquidity are, unsurprisingly, likely to have higher trading costs than long-term, buy-and-hold investment strategies.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The relationship runs the other way — momentum DEMANDS liquidity (buying what's rising), while patient value buying tends to SUPPLY it.

questions.curriculum_ref: Investment Approaches and Effects on Costs — momentum vs value

drills.los_text: describe the potential benefits of shareholder engagement and the role an equity manager might play in shareholder engagement

questions.stem: *Shareholder engagement* is *most likely* defined as:

options.A: Shareholder engagement is defined as purely passive index investing without interaction.
options.B: Shareholder engagement means the buying and selling of shares in secondary markets.
options.C: the process whereby investors actively interact with companies, including voting on corporate matters at general meetings as well as other forms of communication (quarterly investor calls, in-person meetings).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Passivity in trading does not preclude — and is not — engagement.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Trading is portfolio management; engagement is ACTIVE INTERACTION with companies, including voting at general meetings.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the process whereby investors actively interact with companies, including voting on corporate matters at general meetings as well as other forms of communication (quarterly investor calls, in-person meetings) — generally concerning issues that can affect the value of a company and, by extension, an investor's shares.

Correct. The reading defines shareholder engagement as the process whereby investors actively interact with companies. It often includes voting on corporate matters at general meetings as well as other forms of communication (quarterly investor calls, in-person meetings). Generally, engagement concerns issues that can affect the value of a company and, by extension, an investor's shares.

questions.stem: The reading identifies five common topics discussed in shareholder engagement. Which of the following is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: Daily trading strategy of the company's proprietary trading desk.
options.B: Strategy (goals, resources, plans for growth, R&D, culture, sustainability).
options.C: Allocation of capital, corporate governance, remuneration, and composition of the board of directors.

rationales.A: Correct. Proprietary trading is not a topic of shareholder engagement. The five topics the reading identifies are: (1) strategy, (2) allocation of capital (dividends, financial leverage, equity raising, capex), (3) corporate governance and regulatory/political risk (including internal controls and audit/risk committees), (4) remuneration (compensation structures, incentives, alignment), and (5) composition of the board of directors (succession, expertise, culture, diversity, effectiveness).
rationales.B: Strategy is one of the five.
rationales.C: The other four are here.

questions.stem: The reading identifies benefits of shareholder engagement for *both* shareholders and companies. From the company's perspective, engagement *most likely*:

options.A: From the company's perspective, shareholder engagement always harms performance by distracting management.
options.B: can assist in developing a more effective corporate governance culture, which may lead to better company performance to the benefit of shareholders and other stakeholders.
options.C: Engagement eliminates all disagreements between companies and their shareholders.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The reading identifies BENEFITS — engagement can strengthen governance culture.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states from a company's perspective, shareholder engagement can assist in developing a more effective corporate governance culture. In turn, shareholder engagement may lead to better company performance to the benefit of shareholders as well as other stakeholders. Investors also benefit — through more information about companies or the sectors in which they operate.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disagreement persists; the benefit is a more effective governance culture that can improve performance for all shareholders.

questions.stem: Shareholder engagement is *particularly relevant* for which type of investor?

options.A: index-based fund managers, since they can spread engagement costs across large asset bases.
options.B: day traders.
options.C: active portfolio managers, since engagement provides information about company strategy, culture, and competitive environment that informs active investment decisions. By contrast, index-based managers are focused on tracking a benchmark while minimizing costs.

rationales.A: This is partially true for very large index funds where costs can be spread, but active is the primary beneficiary.
rationales.B: Day traders do not engage with companies.
rationales.C:
Full statement: active portfolio managers, since engagement provides information about company strategy, culture, and competitive environment that informs active investment decisions. By contrast, index-based managers are focused on tracking a benchmark while minimizing costs — engagement costs and time detract from that primary goal.

Correct. The reading states shareholder engagement is particularly relevant for active portfolio managers. By contrast, index-based fund managers are primarily focused on tracking a benchmark while minimizing costs to do so. Any process such as shareholder engagement that takes up management time (and adds cost) would detract from the primary goal of an index-based manager. This would be less of an issue for very large index-based portfolios, where engagement costs could be spread over a sizable asset base.

questions.curriculum_ref: Benefits of Shareholder Engagement — active vs index

questions.stem: The *free-rider problem* in shareholder engagement is *most likely*:

options.A: when investors who do not pursue engagement can still benefit from the engagement of others.
options.B: when free public transportation reduces urban stock prices.
options.C: when regulators force shareholders to engage.

rationales.A:
Full statement: when investors who do not pursue engagement can still benefit from the engagement of others — if an engaged active manager successfully increases a company's stock price, other shareholders who did not participate or bear the costs benefit as well.

Correct. The reading defines the free-rider problem: investors who do not pursue engagement can still benefit from the shareholder engagement of others. Specifically, if a portfolio manager using an active strategy actively engages with a company to improve its operations and was successful in increasing the company's stock price, the manager's actions improved the value of his portfolio and also benefited other investors who own the same stock — without those non-engaged investors incurring the costs of engagement.
rationales.B: Not the reading's use of the term.
rationales.C: Not the reading's use of the term.

questions.curriculum_ref: Benefits of Shareholder Engagement — free-rider problem

questions.stem: The reading identifies four *disadvantages* of shareholder engagement. Which of the following is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: Time-consuming and costly for both shareholders and companies.
options.B: Mandatory reporting to the SEC of all engagement discussions.
options.C: Pressure on company management to meet near-term share price or earnings targets at the expense of long-term corporate decisions.

rationales.A: This is one of the four.
rationales.B: Correct. There is no such SEC-reporting requirement. The four disadvantages the reading identifies are: (1) time consuming and costly for both shareholders and companies, (2) pressure on management to meet near-term share price or earnings targets at expense of long-term decisions, (3) selective disclosure of important information to a certain subset of shareholders (which could lead to a breach of insider trading rules), and (4) conflicts of interest for a company (e.g., a portfolio manager engaging with a company that is also an investor in the manager's portfolio).
rationales.C: This is one of the four.

questions.stem: *Activist investing* is *most likely* described as:

options.A: Activist investing is purely passive index tracking with no engagement component.
options.B: Activist investing is always and only focused on management compensation.
options.C: a distinct and specialized version of engagement — activists specialize in taking stakes in companies and creating change to generate a gain on the investment.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Activism is engagement INTENSIFIED, not passivity.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Compensation is one topic among many; activists take stakes to create change that generates investment gains.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a distinct and specialized version of engagement — activists specialize in taking stakes in companies and creating change to generate a gain on the investment; hedge funds are among the most common activists due to potential high performance fees, limited regulation, fewer investment constraints, and ability to leverage positions.

Correct. The reading defines activist investing as a distinct and specialized version of engagement. Activists specialize in taking stakes in companies and creating change to generate a gain on their investment. Hedge funds are among the most common activists, possibly because of the potential for high performance fees. Additionally, because hedge funds are subject to limited regulation, have fewer investment constraints, and can often leverage positions, they often have more flexibility as activists.

questions.stem: Activist engagement tools include meetings with management, shareholder resolutions, letters to management, presentations to other investors, and media campaigns. Activists may also seek *most likely*:

options.A: board representation via proxy contests.
options.B: always to acquire 100% of shares.
options.C: only informal discussions with lower-level employees.

rationales.A:
Full statement: board representation via proxy contests — corporate takeover mechanisms in which shareholders are persuaded to vote for a group seeking a controlling position on the board.

Correct. The reading states activists may seek representation on a company's board of directors as a way of exerting influence. Proxy contests are one method used to obtain board representation. These contests represent corporate takeover mechanisms in which shareholders are persuaded to vote for a group seeking a controlling position on a company's board of directors. Social media and other communication tools can help activists coordinate the actions of other shareholders.
rationales.B: Full acquisition is generally not the activist goal.
rationales.C: Activists engage with management and boards, not lower-level employees.

questions.stem: *General meetings* (general assemblies) are *most likely* important for shareholder engagement because they:

options.A: eliminate all shareholder rights.
options.B: are among the most influential tools available for shareholder engagement.
options.C: are open only to management and directors.

rationales.A: General meetings preserve, not eliminate, rights.
rationales.B:
Full statement: are among the most influential tools available for shareholder engagement — enabling shareholders to participate in discussions and vote on major corporate matters and transactions that are not delegated to the board of directors.

Correct. The reading states the participation of shareholders in general meetings (also known as general assemblies), and the exercise of their voting rights, are among the most influential tools available for shareholder engagement. General meetings enable shareholders to participate in discussions and to vote on major corporate matters and transactions that are not delegated to the board of directors.
rationales.C: General meetings include shareholders.

questions.stem: *Proxy voting* is *most likely* described as:

options.A: Proxy voting is prohibited under most jurisdictions' corporate law.
options.B: Proxy voting refers to voting conducted via cryptocurrency tokens.
options.C: enabling shareholders unable to attend a meeting to authorize another individual (another shareholder or director) to vote on their behalf.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Proxy voting is standard, legally supported practice worldwide.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The proxy is a PERSON — another shareholder or director authorized to vote for a shareholder unable to attend.
rationales.C:
Full statement: enabling shareholders unable to attend a meeting to authorize another individual (another shareholder or director) to vote on their behalf — the most common form of investor participation in general meetings.

Correct. The reading defines proxy voting as enabling shareholders who are unable to attend a meeting to authorize another individual (e.g., another shareholder or director) to vote on their behalf. Proxy voting is the most common form of investor participation in general meetings. Although most resolutions pass without controversy, sometimes minority shareholders attempt to strengthen their influence via proxy voting. Multiple shareholders may collectively vote in favor of or in opposition to a resolution.

questions.stem: The reading notes *external proxy advisory firms* (e.g., Institutional Shareholder Services and Glass Lewis) *most likely*:

options.A: provide voting recommendations and reduce research efforts by investors.
options.B: make voting decisions binding on all shareholders.
options.C: prohibit portfolio managers from voting.

rationales.A:
Full statement: provide voting recommendations and reduce research efforts by investors — portfolio managers need not follow the recommendations but these external parties can highlight potential controversial issues.

Correct. The reading states some investors use external proxy advisory firms such as Institutional Shareholder Services and Glass Lewis that provide voting recommendations and reduce research efforts by investors. Portfolio managers need not follow the recommendations of proxy advisers, but these external parties can highlight potential controversial issues. An investor's voting instructions are typically processed electronically via third-party proxy voting agents.
rationales.B: These firms advise; they do not bind.
rationales.C: PMs retain voting discretion.

questions.stem: When an investor *loans shares*, the reading notes an important implication for voting rights: *most likely*:

options.A: When shares are loaned, the voting rights remain with the lender at all times, since a securities loan transfers only economic exposure rather than legal title.
options.B: the transaction is technically an assignment of title with a repurchase option — voting rights are transferred to the borrower — which could potentially result in the borrower having different voting opinions from the lending investor.
options.C: When shares are loaned, the voting rights are permanently extinguished for the duration of the loan, with neither the lender nor the borrower entitled to vote them.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Title transfers with the loan — and voting rights travel with title to the borrower.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the transaction is technically an assignment of title with a repurchase option — voting rights are transferred to the borrower — which could potentially result in the borrower having different voting opinions from the lending investor; some stock lenders therefore recall shares ahead of voting resolutions.

Correct. The reading states when an investor loans shares, the transaction is technically an assignment of title with a repurchase option — voting rights are transferred to the borrower. The transfer could potentially result in the borrower having different voting opinions from the lending investor. To mitigate this, some stock lenders recall shares ahead of voting resolutions. The downside is loss of stock lending revenue during the recall period and potential reputation risk. In some cases, investors borrow shares explicitly to exercise voting rights ('empty voting') — no capital invested but voting rights acquired.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Rights transfer, not vanish; lenders wanting to vote must recall shares before the record date.

questions.curriculum_ref: Voting — stock lending and voting rights

drills.los_text: describe rationales for equity investment across the active management spectrum

questions.stem: The reading argues that the decision between index-based and active equity management is *most likely*:

options.A: a strict either/or (binary) choice.
options.B: irrelevant to portfolio construction.
options.C: not an either/or decision.

rationales.A: The reading explicitly rejects the binary framing.
rationales.B: This is a central portfolio construction decision.
rationales.C:
Full statement: not an either/or decision — equity portfolios tend to exist on a spectrum ranging from portfolios that closely track a broad market-cap-weighted index to concentrated portfolios of a few equity securities.

Correct. The reading states the debate over index-based and active management has been longstanding. In reality, the decision is not an 'either/or' (binary) alternative. Instead, equity portfolios tend to exist on a spectrum, ranging from portfolios that closely track a broad market-cap-weighted index to concentrated portfolios of a few equity securities. In some cases, portfolios may resemble a 'closet index' — advertised as actively managed but essentially an index fund.

questions.curriculum_ref: Equity Investment across the Active Management Spectrum

questions.stem: The reading identifies six considerations for positioning a portfolio along the active management spectrum. Which of the following is *least likely* one of them?

options.A: Marketing budget for the fund's distribution.
options.B: Confidence to outperform, client preference, suitable benchmark.
options.C: Client-specific mandates, risks/costs of active management, taxes.

rationales.A: Correct. Marketing budget is not one of the six spectrum-positioning considerations. The six are: (1) confidence to outperform, (2) client preference, (3) suitable benchmark, (4) client-specific mandates, (5) risks/costs of active management, and (6) taxes.
rationales.B: These are three of the six.
rationales.C: These are three of the six.

questions.stem: *Confidence to outperform* requires the active manager to have *most likely*:

options.A: Confidence to outperform requires no fundamental research capability on the manager's part.
options.B: an understanding of the manager's equity investment universe, a competitive analysis of other managers with a similar universe, and appropriate resources (research staff and access to information).
options.C: Confidence to outperform requires a contractual guarantee of alpha delivery.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Research capability is a required foundation.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading states that active investment managers typically need to be confident they can adequately outperform their benchmark. This requires an understanding of the manager's equity investment universe, a competitive analysis of other managers with a similar universe, and appropriate resources (e.g., research staff and access to information). Without these, outperformance confidence is unfounded.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Alpha cannot be guaranteed; the requirement is universe understanding, competitive analysis of similar managers, and demonstrable insight.

questions.curriculum_ref: Active Management Spectrum — Confidence to Outperform

questions.stem: The reading notes that in *large-cap developed markets* investors often believe potential alpha is *most likely* reduced because:

options.A: companies are less known.
options.B: regulation prohibits active management.
options.C: companies are widely known and have considerable equity analyst coverage.

rationales.A: This reverses the actual dynamic.
rationales.B: Active management is legal — just harder to justify.
rationales.C:
Full statement: companies are widely known and have considerable equity analyst coverage — so all publicly available information is efficiently disseminated, analyzed, and reflected in stock prices.

Correct. The reading states in some equity market categories, such as large-cap/developed markets, companies are widely known and have considerable equity analyst coverage. For such categories, investors often believe that potential alpha is substantially reduced because all publicly available information is efficiently disseminated, analyzed, and reflected in stock prices. This drives many large-cap DM investors toward index-based approaches.

questions.stem: Exhibit 9 in the reading (Morningstar data as of Oct 31, 2021) shows the prevalence of index-based vs active management by category. It shows *most likely*:

options.A: categories differ widely — nearly all AUM in categories like foreign small/mid-cap growth is active, while other categories like large-cap blend are predominantly index-based.
options.B: nearly all US open-end fund and ETF AUM is index-based across all categories.
options.C: all categories are 50/50 active/index.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading's Exhibit 9 shows nearly all AUM in some categories (such as foreign small/mid growth) is managed on an active basis, while other categories (such as large-cap blend) are predominantly managed using an index-based approach. Categories vary widely across the spectrum from nearly all active to nearly all passive.
rationales.B: Active still dominates in many categories.
rationales.C: This is not the empirical pattern.

questions.stem: *Suitable benchmark* considerations for active managers *most likely* require:

options.A: Suitable benchmarks for active managers carry no liquidity requirement for underlying securities.
options.B: benchmarks with sufficient liquidity of underlying securities (thus maintaining a reasonable cost of trading), and enough underlying securities to generate sufficient alpha.
options.C: A single-stock benchmark is suitable for a diversified active mandate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Liquidity keeps trading costs reasonable — a benchmark requirement, not an option.
rationales.B:
Full statement: benchmarks with sufficient liquidity of underlying securities (thus maintaining a reasonable cost of trading), and enough underlying securities to generate sufficient alpha — for this reason, many country-specific or sector-specific investment strategies (e.g., consumer defensive companies) are index based.

Correct. The reading states an investor or equity manager's choice of benchmark can play a meaningful role in the ability to attract new funds. As part of the selection process, active managers normally must have benchmarks with sufficient liquidity of underlying securities (thus maintaining reasonable cost of trading). In addition, the number of securities underlying the benchmark typically must be broad enough to generate sufficient alpha. For this reason, many country-specific or sector-specific investment strategies (e.g., consumer defensive companies) are index-based.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Diversified investing requires ENOUGH underlying securities; one stock supports neither diversification nor meaningful active positioning.

questions.stem: *Client-specific mandates* related to ESG considerations are *most likely*:

options.A: Client-specific ESG mandates are impossible to implement in practice.
options.B: Client-specific ESG mandates are always managed passively through screened indexes.
options.C: typically managed actively, because index-based management may not be particularly efficient or cost effective when managers must meet client-desired holdings or holdings to avoid.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Such mandates are widely implemented.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The reading identifies them as typically ACTIVE — bespoke exclusions and integration make index-based approaches inefficient.
rationales.C:
Full statement: typically managed actively, because index-based management may not be particularly efficient or cost effective when managers must meet client-desired holdings or holdings to avoid — for example, exclusionary/negative screening requires ongoing monitoring to determine companies directly and indirectly involved in 'unacceptable' industries.

Correct. The reading states client-specific investment mandates, such as those related to ESG considerations, are typically managed actively. This active approach occurs because index-based management may not be particularly efficient or cost effective when managers must meet a client's desired holdings (or holdings to avoid). For example, a mandate to avoid certain 'unacceptable' activities (military technology/weapons, tobacco/alcohol, gambling) requires ongoing monitoring — managers need to determine which companies are directly and indirectly involved. Although ESG investing is typically active, a growing number of index-based ESG vehicles exist.

questions.stem: *Key-person risk* is *most likely*:

options.A: a risk of active management if the success of an investment manager's firm is dependent on one or a few 'star managers' who may potentially leave the firm.
options.B: Key-person risk is a distinctive risk of index-based management.
options.C: Key-person risk refers to the risk of losing the fund's key legal counsel.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading identifies key-person risk as a risk of active management. Key-person risk is relevant for active managers if the success of an investment manager's firm is dependent on one or a few individuals ('star managers') who may potentially leave the firm. This is generally less of a concern for index-based strategies where portfolio management is rules-based.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Rules-based indexing depends little on individual talent; the risk attaches to ACTIVE management built on star managers.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The 'key persons' are the star investment managers whose departure threatens the franchise.

questions.stem: In *tax* considerations for choosing between active and index-based management, the reading notes *most likely*:

options.A: active strategies always minimize taxes better than index-based.
options.B: index-based strategies generally have lower turnover and generate a higher percentage of long-term gains — an index fund that replicates its benchmark can have minimal rebalancing.
options.C: tax considerations are identical for all investors globally.

rationales.A: The reading identifies the opposite general tendency.
rationales.B:
Full statement: index-based strategies generally have lower turnover and generate a higher percentage of long-term gains — an index fund that replicates its benchmark can have minimal rebalancing; active strategies can be designed to minimize tax consequences but at the expense of higher trading costs.

Correct. The reading states compared with active strategies, index-based strategies generally have lower turnover and generate a higher percentage of long-term gains. An index fund that replicates its benchmark can have minimal rebalancing. In turn, active strategies can be designed to minimize tax consequences of gains/income at the expense of higher trading costs. One overall challenge is that tax legislation differs widely among countries.
rationales.C: Tax legislation differs widely by country.

questions.stem: For a cost-conscious client who believes equity markets are highly efficient and has a narrow investment focus in specific country and industry sectors, the reading's expected positioning along the active management spectrum is *most likely*:

options.A: For the cost-conscious efficiency-believing client, highly concentrated active management is the recommendation.
options.B: For this client, a long/short hedge fund structure is the recommendation.
options.C: index-based, because the client believes in market efficiency (implying alpha is difficult to earn), values low costs, and narrow country/sector focus is typically well-served by index-based products.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Belief in efficiency undercuts the case for concentrated active risk — and the costs contradict her priorities.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Long/short is high-cost active management; index-based exposure fits her efficiency view, cost sensitivity, and narrow focus.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading's Goudreaux example: cost consciousness and belief in market efficiency drive her toward index-based management. Because she believes in market efficiency, she likely believes it is challenging for active managers to outperform. Because she is cost conscious, index-based (typically lower fee) is attractive. Her narrow country/sector focus is well-served by index-based sector or country ETFs. So her portfolio is likely index-based.

questions.stem: A *closet index* fund is *most likely*:

options.A: a portfolio advertised as actively managed but essentially an index fund.
options.B: an index fund advertised as an active fund.
options.C: an index fund with a hidden name.

rationales.A:
Full statement: a portfolio advertised as actively managed but essentially an index fund — meaning the client is paying active fees for what is effectively index performance.

Correct. The reading states in some cases portfolios may resemble a 'closet index' in which the portfolio is advertised as actively managed but is essentially an index fund. This is problematic for clients who are paying active management fees but effectively receiving index performance — a mismatch of value delivered to fees paid.
rationales.B: This reverses the meaning.
rationales.C: Not the reading's meaning.

questions.stem: The reading notes that many active equity portfolio managers have historically been *most likely*:

options.A: consistently profitable at beating benchmarks after fees.
options.B: unsuccessful at beating benchmarks and have charged high management fees, driving increased popularity of index-based strategies.
options.C: prohibited from operating.

rationales.A: This reverses the empirical record the reading cites.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading's summary states many active equity portfolio managers are unsuccessful at beating benchmarks and have charged high management fees. Consequently, index-based strategies have increased in popularity. This structural underperformance vs benchmarks post-fees is a key driver of the shift toward passive over recent decades.
rationales.C: Active management is legal.

questions.curriculum_ref: Active Management Spectrum — active manager performance

drills.los_text: discuss considerations in choosing a benchmark for an equity portfolio

questions.stem: For an index to become the basis for an equity investment strategy, the reading identifies three initial requirements. These are *most likely*:

options.A: cap-weighted, US-listed, and dividend-paying.
options.B: actively managed, low-cost, and diversified.
options.C: rules-based, transparent, and investable.

rationales.A: These are not the three requirements.
rationales.B: These are not the three requirements.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading states for an index to become the basis for an equity investment strategy, it must meet three initial requirements: it must be (1) rules-based, (2) transparent, and (3) investable. Rules are objective, consistent, and predictable. Transparency ensures that benchmark providers disclose the rules and constituents without any 'black-box' methodologies, assuring investors the index will continue to represent the intended strategy.

questions.curriculum_ref: Benchmark Selection — Indexes for Index-Based Strategies

questions.stem: The reading identifies two examples of *non-investable* indexes. These are *most likely*:

options.A: hedge fund-of-funds indexes (e.g., the HFRI series.
options.B: the FTSE 100 and the S&P 500.
options.C: no equity index is non-investable.

rationales.A:
Full statement: hedge fund-of-funds indexes (e.g., the HFRI series — difficult to buy the constituent hedge funds) and the Value Line Geometric Index (a multiplicative-average price index that cannot be replicated).

Correct. The reading cites these two non-investable indexes: (1) the HFRI series of hedge fund-of-funds indexes — most investors cannot track them because of the difficulty of buying the constituent hedge funds; (2) the Value Line Geometric Index — the value of the index is obtained by multiplying the prices and taking a root corresponding to the number of stocks; this multiplicative-average approach is not useful for investing purposes because it cannot be replicated. In contrast, the FTSE 100 is investable because its constituent securities can be purchased easily on the London Stock Exchange.
rationales.B: Both are highly investable.
rationales.C: Some indexes are indeed non-investable.

questions.stem: *Free-float adjustment* of index shares outstanding is *most likely*:

options.A: Free-float adjustment counts every share ever issued without distinction.
options.B: counting only shares available for trade by the public, excluding shares that are held by founders, governments, or other companies — because closely held or strategic-holder shares are seldom traded and could cause disproportionate trading effects.
options.C: Free-float adjustment is the adjustment of share counts for dilution from options and convertibles.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Counting everything is the UNADJUSTED total; float adjustment removes strategic holdings.
rationales.B:
Full statement: counting only shares available for trade by the public, excluding shares that are held by founders, governments, or other companies — because closely held or strategic-holder shares are seldom traded and could cause disproportionate trading effects; float-adjustment relies on publicly available information.

Correct. The reading states many stock indexes 'free-float adjust' their shares outstanding, which means they count only shares available for trade by the public, excluding those held by founders, governments, or other companies. When a company's floated shares are a small fraction of total shares outstanding, trading can result in disproportionate effects. Similarly, stocks for which trading volume is a small fraction of total shares outstanding are likely to have low liquidity and high trading costs. Many indexes require float and average shares traded above a certain percentage.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Dilution is a different adjustment; float excludes founder, government, and corporate cross-holdings unavailable for public trading.

questions.stem: The reading identifies five major equity *index providers*. These include CRSP, FTSE Russell, Morningstar, MSCI, and *most likely*:

options.A: the New York Stock Exchange.
options.B: Nasdaq only.
options.C: S&P Dow Jones.

rationales.A: NYSE is an exchange, not primarily an index provider.
rationales.B: Nasdaq operates the Nasdaq family of indexes but is not among the five broad providers the reading names.
rationales.C: Correct. The reading lists five major equity index providers: CRSP, FTSE Russell, Morningstar, MSCI, and S&P Dow Jones. These index providers publicize the rules underlying their indexes, communicate changes in constituent securities, and report performance. For a fee, they may also provide data to investors who want to replicate the underlying basket of securities.

questions.stem: *Buffering* — an index provider's method to limit stock migration between indexes — is *most likely*:

options.A: establishing ranges around breakpoints that define whether a stock belongs in one index or another; as long as stocks remain within the buffer zone, they stay in their current index.
options.B: the immediate reclassification of every stock whenever its market cap changes.
options.C: prohibiting index changes ever.

rationales.A: Correct. The reading defines buffering as establishing ranges around breakpoints that define whether a stock belongs in one index or another. As long as stocks remain within the buffer zone, they stay in their current index. Example: the MSCI USA Large Cap Index contains the 300 largest US companies; a mid-cap stock must achieve a rank as the 200th largest to move UP to Large Cap Index, and a large-cap constituent must shrink and be the 451st largest to move DOWN to Mid Cap Index. Since size rankings can change daily, buffering makes transitions more gradual and orderly.
rationales.B: This would create excessive turnover.
rationales.C: Buffering allows changes but under controlled conditions.

questions.stem: In the MSCI USA Large Cap Index buffering example, *Whole Foods Market* was moved from the Large Cap Index to the Mid Cap Index at May 2016 rebalance because *most likely*:

options.A: Whole Foods moved from the Large Cap to the Mid Cap index because the company changed its industry classification.
