options.B: its market cap dropped from USD15.3 billion (May 2015) to USD10.4 billion (May 2016), below the buffer value of approximately USD10.5 billion (67% of the USD15,707 million cutoff for the smallest company in the Large Cap Index).
options.C: Whole Foods moved indexes because the company was delisted from public markets.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Industry was unchanged; SIZE was the trigger.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading's Whole Foods Market example: MSCI sets a cutoff value for the smallest company in the Large Cap Index at each rebalance and sets the buffer at 67% of the cutoff. In May 2016, the cutoff market cap was USD15,707M; buffer was USD10,524M (~USD10.5B). Whole Foods' market cap had dropped from USD15.3B (May 2015) to USD10.4B (May 2016), below the USD10.5B buffer. Per the stated rules, Whole Foods was removed from MSCI USA Large Cap and added to MSCI USA Mid Cap Index.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The stock continued trading until the 2017 Amazon acquisition; the 2016 move followed its market cap falling from $15.3B to $10.4B, through the buffer.

questions.curriculum_ref: Benchmark Selection — Whole Foods Market example

questions.stem: *Packeting* — an alternative approach to controlling stock migration between indexes — is *most likely*:

options.A: Packeting is the immediate transfer of all of a stock's shares upon any breakpoint breach.
options.B: Packeting prohibits a stock from ever appearing in more than one index simultaneously.
options.C: splitting stock positions into multiple parts: if a mid-cap stock's cap increases and breaches the mid-cap/large-cap breakpoint, a portion of the total holding is transferred to the large-cap index but the rest stays in the mid-cap index.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Immediate full transfer is the conventional approach packeting replaces.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Packeting EXPLICITLY permits split membership — a portion moves on the first breach, the remainder on confirmation at the next review.
rationales.C:
Full statement: splitting stock positions into multiple parts: if a mid-cap stock's cap increases and breaches the mid-cap/large-cap breakpoint, a portion of the total holding is transferred to the large-cap index but the rest stays in the mid-cap index — the remainder moves on the next reconstitution if the stock still qualifies. This keeps portfolio turnover and trading costs low.

Correct. The reading defines packeting as splitting stock positions into multiple parts. If a stock's capitalization breaches the mid-cap/large-cap breakpoint, a portion of the total holding is transferred to the large-cap index but the rest stays in the mid-cap index. On the next reconstitution date, if the stock's value remains large cap and all other qualifications are met, the remainder of the shares are moved. This policy keeps portfolio turnover and trading costs low. CRSP uses packeting for the CRSP family of indexes.

questions.stem: The first consideration when choosing a benchmark index is *most likely*:

options.A: the desired factor exposures.
options.B: the fund manager's biography.
options.C: the exchange where the index is quoted.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the desired factor exposures — driven by the IPS objectives and constraints (geographic market, size, style, and other risk factors such as momentum, low volatility, and quality).

Correct. The reading states the first consideration when choosing a benchmark index is the desired factor exposures, which is driven by the objectives and constraints in the investor's IPS. Choices include: (1) geographic market segment (broad vs sectors, domestic vs international), (2) size (large, mid, or small market cap), (3) style (value, growth, blend/core), and (4) other constituent characteristics (high or low momentum, low volatility, quality) — considered risk factors.
rationales.B: Manager biography is not the primary benchmark consideration.
rationales.C: Exchange listing is a much less relevant consideration.

questions.stem: The reading identifies market-cap-weighted indexes as having a theoretical foundation because *most likely*:

options.A: the equally-weighted market portfolio is CAPM-optimal.
options.B: in CAPM, the capitalization-weighted market portfolio is mean-variance efficient, offering the highest return for a given level of risk.
options.C: cap-weighting eliminates all portfolio risk.

rationales.A: This reverses CAPM logic.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in CAPM, the capitalization-weighted market portfolio is mean-variance efficient, offering the highest return for a given level of risk; to the extent a cap-weighted equity index is a reasonable proxy for the market portfolio, the tracking portfolio may be close to mean-variance efficient. Cap weighting can also be thought of as liquidity weighting — largest-cap stocks tend to have highest liquidity and capacity to handle investor flows at manageable cost.

Correct. The reading states in the development of CAPM, the cap-weighted market portfolio is mean-variance efficient, meaning it offers the highest return for a given level of risk. To the extent a cap-weighted equity index is a reasonable proxy for the market portfolio, the tracking portfolio may be close to mean-variance efficient. A further advantage is that it reflects a strategy's investment capacity — a cap-weighted index can be thought of as a liquidity-weighted index because largest-cap stocks tend to have the highest liquidity.
rationales.C: Cap-weighting doesn't eliminate market risk.

questions.stem: *Equally weighted* indexes are *most likely* characterized by:

options.A: no rebalancing ever required.
options.B: no exposure to any factor.
options.C: the least-concentrated portfolios, with constituent weights of 1/n.

rationales.A: Equal-weight requires very active rebalancing.
rationales.B: Zeng-Luo (2013) notes broad equally-weighted indexes are factor indifferent, but they impart a small-cap bias.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the least-concentrated portfolios, with constituent weights of 1/n; imparting a small-cap bias and higher volatility than cap-weighting; requiring regular rebalancing (weights drift immediately after trading begins); and having limited investment capacity because smallest-cap constituents may have low liquidity.

Correct. The reading states equally weighted indexes produce the least-concentrated portfolios with constituent weights of 1/n. Equal weighting can be considered a naive strategy but reduces single-stock concentration and slowly changes sector exposures. Equal weighting produces higher volatility than cap weighting, partly because it imparts a small-cap bias. Equal weights deviate most from market weights for large-cap indexes containing mega-cap stocks. Equally weighted indexes require regular rebalancing since weights are no longer equal immediately after trading begins. A drawback is limited investment capacity due to low liquidity of smallest-cap constituents.

questions.stem: The reading distinguishes *market-cap-weighted* and *fundamentally weighted* indexes based on their underlying philosophies. Which is *most likely*?

options.A: Market-cap-weighted portfolios are based on the efficient market hypothesis.
options.B: Both approaches are based on identical assumptions.
options.C: Both approaches assume market inefficiency.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Market-cap-weighted portfolios are based on the efficient market hypothesis; fundamentally weighted indexes look to exploit possible inefficiencies in market pricing (weight based on such attributes as sales, income, or dividends — delinking a stock's portfolio weight from its market value).

Correct. The reading states market-cap-weighted indexes and fundamentally weighted indexes share attractive characteristics (low cost, rule-based construction, transparency, investability), but their philosophies are different. Market-cap-weighted portfolios are based on the efficient market hypothesis, while fundamentally weighted indexes look to exploit possible inefficiencies in market pricing. Fundamental weighting delinks a constituent stock's portfolio weight from its market value — the philosophy is that although stock prices may become over- or undervalued, the market price will eventually converge to a level implied by the fundamental attributes.
rationales.B: The philosophies differ fundamentally.
rationales.C: Only fundamental weighting assumes inefficiency.

questions.stem: The reading uses the *Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)* to measure index concentration. Given the *effective number of stocks* = 1/HHI, and HHI = Σw²ᵢ, if a market-cap-weighted index's top-5 stocks square-sum to 0.02603 and bottom-45 stocks square-sum to 0.01405, giving total HHI = 0.04008, the effective number of stocks is *closest to*:

rationales.A: Too low.
rationales.B: Correct. The reading's Example: HHI = 0.04008, and Effective number of stocks = 1/HHI = 1/0.04008 = 24.95, or approximately 25. This is well below the nominal 50-stock count of the index, indicating meaningful concentration among the top holdings. The reading notes cap-weighted indexes often have a surprisingly low effective number of stocks — e.g., the NASDAQ 100 in May 2017 had 100 stocks but an effective number of about 25.
rationales.C: This is the nominal number of stocks, not the effective number.

questions.curriculum_ref: Benchmark Selection — HHI effective number of stocks

questions.stem: The reading notes that *reconstitution* of an index (adding/deleting constituents) can create *most likely*:

options.A: no price effects because markets are perfectly efficient.
options.B: no relevance to index-fund investors.
options.C: significant price effects — demand for newly-included names pushes up their prices while depressing prices of deleted ones.

rationales.A: Reconstitution effects are documented.
rationales.B: Reconstitution effects create hidden costs for index investors.
rationales.C:
Full statement: significant price effects — demand for newly-included names pushes up their prices while depressing prices of deleted ones; arbitrageurs may anticipate objective-criteria changes and front-run trades, so committee-based subjective reconstitution decisions are harder to predict; Petajisto (2010) showed Russell 2000-→ Russell 1000 movements associate with negative effects while movements the other direction tend to increase stock prices.

Correct. The reading states when reconstitution occurs, index-tracking portfolios/mutual funds/ETFs want to hold newly included names and sell deleted names. Demand can push up prices of added companies while depressing prices of deleted ones. Arbitrageurs may be able to anticipate objective-criteria changes and front-run. Committee-based subjective decisions are harder to predict. Chen, Noronha, and Singal (2004) found subjective reconstitutions are harder to arbitrage. Petajisto (2010) showed process of moving from Russell 1000 to Russell 2000 associates with stock price increases; movements to large-cap index tend to have negative effects. Transparency in reconstitution is a virtue.
